levers.name: Decouple AltID from Apple/Google
levers.consequences: Removing the single point of failure in the current MitID setup will reduce Denmark's digital sovereignty risk, but it may also increase complexity and costs for public services that currently rely on platform-controlled signals.
options: Implement a separate identity verification system using open standards like FIDO2/WebAuthn
Develop a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path to reduce dependence on mobile OS duopoly
Establish a national digital identity hub that aggregates and verifies user identities across multiple platforms
levers.review_lever: Decoupling AltID from Apple/Google will create trade-offs between complexity and cost, but the project should focus on establishing a platform-neutral fallback access path to reduce Denmark's digital sovereignty risk.

levers.name: Influence EU Digital Identity Wallet framework
levers.consequences: Shaping European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in will have a direct impact on Danish digital identity planning, but it may also require significant coordination with EU bodies and technical working groups.
options: Engage with relevant EU bodies to include platform-neutral access requirements in the Digital Identity Wallet framework
Develop a technical proposal for a European-level fallback authentication mechanism that can be adopted by multiple member states
Establish a coalition of Nordic countries to advocate for common digital identity standards and policies
levers.review_lever: Influencing the EU Digital Identity Wallet framework will introduce trade-offs between coordination and impact, but the project should focus on shaping European requirements that support platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms.

levers.name: Secure formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen
levers.consequences: Obtaining a formal written response will demonstrate progress toward the project's objective, but it may also require significant engagement with relevant authorities and stakeholders.
options: Develop a policy proposal that addresses the feasibility and risk report, as well as the fallback-authentication concept note
Establish a working group with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to discuss and refine the proposal
Organize a public consultation to gather feedback from relevant stakeholders
levers.review_lever: Securing a formal written response will create trade-offs between engagement and progress, but the project should focus on demonstrating progress toward its objective.

levers.name: Build credibility through demonstrator work
levers.consequences: Producing credible technical evidence will increase the project's influence on digital identity planning, but it may also require significant investment in non-production demonstrators.
options: Develop a mobile-oriented demonstrator that authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards like FIDO2/WebAuthn
Explore a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path to reduce dependence on the mobile OS duopoly
Invest in a hybrid approach that combines elements of both demonstrators
levers.review_lever: Building credibility through demonstrator work will introduce trade-offs between investment and influence, but the project should focus on producing credible technical evidence that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms.

levers.name: Establish a coalition of stakeholders
levers.consequences: Building a coalition will increase the project's influence and credibility, but it may also require significant coordination and engagement with relevant stakeholders.
options: Engage with civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee
Develop a coalition-building strategy that focuses on key influencers and decision-makers
Organize regular stakeholder meetings to discuss progress and gather feedback
levers.review_lever: Establishing a coalition will create trade-offs between coordination and influence, but the project should focus on building a broad coalition of stakeholders.

levers.name: Secure funding for phase two
levers.consequences: Obtaining funding for phase two will allow the project to proceed with demonstrator work, but it may also require significant engagement with funders and stakeholders.
options: Develop a detailed financing plan that covers the remainder of the project
Engage with potential funders to discuss and refine the proposal
Organize a public consultation to gather feedback from relevant stakeholders
levers.review_lever: Securing funding for phase two will introduce trade-offs between engagement and progress, but the project should focus on demonstrating progress toward its objective.

levers.name: Influence procurement processes around AltID
levers.consequences: Shaping procurement requirements will have a direct impact on Danish digital identity planning, but it may also require significant coordination with relevant authorities and stakeholders.
options: Develop a policy proposal that addresses the feasibility and risk report, as well as the fallback-authentication concept note
Establish a working group with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to discuss and refine the proposal
Organize a public consultation to gather feedback from relevant stakeholders
levers.review_lever: Influencing procurement processes will create trade-offs between coordination and impact, but the project should focus on shaping requirements that support platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms.

levers.name: Build a strong technical team
levers.consequences: Assembling a strong technical team will increase the project's credibility and influence, but it may also require significant investment in staffing and training.
options: Hire a lead researcher in mobile security and authentication protocols
Recruit a regulatory and compliance specialist familiar with Danish and EU digital identity frameworks
Invest in a technical lead for demonstrator work and additional engineers or contractors
levers.review_lever: Building a strong technical team will introduce trade-offs between investment and credibility, but the project should focus on assembling a diverse and experienced team.

levers.name: Engage the Danish Parliament's IT Committee
levers.consequences: Securing a formal hearing before the committee can validate the project's platform-neutrality and fallback-access goals, but may also attract scrutiny from Apple/Google lobbyists.
options: Testify with technical evidence on the feasibility of platform-neutral digital identity
Lobby for explicit language in parliamentary reports highlighting supplier-concentration risks
Collaborate with committee members to draft legislation requiring platform-neutrality in Danish public services
levers.review_lever: Engaging the Danish Parliament's IT Committee introduces a trade-off between securing formal validation and facing scrutiny from Apple/Google lobbyists. This lever leaves unaddressed the potential for coalition-building efforts to be overshadowed by more vocal or well-funded interests.

levers.name: Develop a Proof-of-Concept (PoC) on FIDO2/WebAuthn
levers.consequences: A successful PoC can demonstrate the feasibility of platform-neutral authentication, but may also inadvertently create unrealistic expectations about deployability or scalability.
options: Implement a minimal viable product (MVP) that authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment
Explore the use of FIDO2/WebAuthn with a focus on device-agnostic and platform-neutral authentication flows
Develop a PoC that integrates FIDO2/WebAuthn with existing MitID infrastructure, highlighting potential for incremental upgrades
levers.review_lever: Developing a Proof-of-Concept on FIDO2/WebAuthn introduces a trade-off between demonstrating feasibility and creating unrealistic expectations about deployability or scalability. This lever leaves unaddressed the need to prioritize coalition-building efforts over technical innovation.

levers.name: Influence EU Digital Identity Wallet framework
levers.consequences: Securing language in the EU's Digital Identity Wallet framework that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms can have significant implications for Denmark's digital identity strategy, but may also require compromise on core goals.
options: Advocate for explicit language requiring member states to implement platform-neutral authentication paths
Collaborate with EU bodies to develop guidelines and best practices for platform-neutral digital identity
Lobby for inclusion of fallback-access requirements in the EU's Digital Identity Wallet framework
levers.review_lever: Influencing the EU Digital Identity Wallet framework introduces a trade-off between securing language that supports platform-neutral access and requiring compromise on core goals. This lever leaves unaddressed the need to prioritize coalition-building efforts over EU-level engagement.

levers.name: Build a Stronger Coalition with Civil Society
levers.consequences: A more robust coalition can amplify the project's voice and influence, but may also create tensions or conflicts within the group.
options: Engage with civil society organizations to co-create awareness campaigns highlighting supplier-concentration risks
Collaborate with advocacy groups to draft policy briefs that emphasize platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements
Host workshops and roundtables to build consensus among coalition members on key goals and messaging
levers.review_lever: Building a stronger coalition with civil society introduces a trade-off between amplifying the project's voice and influence, and creating tensions or conflicts within the group. This lever leaves unaddressed the need to prioritize technical innovation over coalition-building efforts.

levers.name: Develop a Fallback-Access Demonstration
levers.consequences: A successful demonstration can showcase the feasibility of fallback-access, but may also highlight the complexity and challenges associated with implementing such a system.
options: Implement a minimal viable product (MVP) that authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using FIDO2/WebAuthn
Explore the use of hardware tokens or browser-based fallback-access methods, highlighting potential for incremental upgrades to MitID infrastructure
Develop a demonstration that integrates fallback-access with existing MitID infrastructure, emphasizing potential for incremental upgrades
levers.review_lever: Developing a fallback-access demonstration introduces a trade-off between showcasing feasibility and highlighting complexity and challenges. This lever leaves unaddressed the need to prioritize coalition-building efforts over technical innovation.

levers.name: Engage with the European Digital Identity Association
levers.consequences: Securing membership or influence within the association can provide a platform for amplifying the project's voice and goals, but may also require compromise on core objectives.
options: Join the association as an individual member to contribute to discussions on digital identity standards and best practices
Collaborate with the association to develop guidelines and recommendations for platform-neutral digital identity
Lobby for inclusion of fallback-access requirements in the association's policy documents
levers.review_lever: Engaging with the European Digital Identity Association introduces a trade-off between amplifying the project's voice and goals, and requiring compromise on core objectives. This lever leaves unaddressed the need to prioritize coalition-building efforts over EU-level engagement.

levers.name: Reframe Danish Public Sector Identity Policy
levers.consequences: This lever directly impacts how the Danish public sector approaches digital identity, shifting focus from convenience to resilience and supplier concentration risk.
options: Embed platform-neutrality in policy frameworks
Incorporate continuity of service into national eIDAS2 requirements
Develop a formalized procurement process for fallback authentication protocols
levers.review_lever: This lever introduces the primary trade-off between convenience and resilience, but leaves unaddressed the gap in technical standards and certification processes that need to be adapted for platform-neutral access.

levers.name: Develop a Fallback Authentication Standard
levers.consequences: This lever creates a new standard for fallback authentication, which can reduce dependence on Apple/Google but introduces the risk of vendor lock-in with the chosen standard.
options: Adopt an open standards approach using FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect
Develop a proprietary standard for fallback authentication
Collaborate with EU bodies to create a harmonized European digital identity wallet standard
levers.review_lever: This lever introduces the trade-off between vendor lock-in and standardization, but leaves unaddressed the gap in how this new standard will be adopted by existing operators.

levers.name: Influence AltID Procurement Processes
levers.consequences: This lever directly impacts the procurement processes for AltID, ensuring that platform-neutral and fallback-capable solutions are considered, but may introduce delays due to institutional inertia.
options: Embed platform-neutrality in AltID tender requirements
Develop a separate RFP process for fallback authentication protocols
Collaborate with EU bodies to create harmonized procurement standards
levers.review_lever: This lever introduces the trade-off between institutional inertia and procurement influence, but leaves unaddressed the gap in how this new approach will be received by existing operators.

levers.name: Establish a Fallback Authentication Demonstrator
levers.consequences: This lever creates a working demonstrator for fallback authentication, which can provide credible technical evidence but may not directly influence procurement processes.
options: Develop a mobile-oriented demonstrator using open standards
Create a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path
Collaborate with EU bodies to develop a harmonized European digital identity wallet demonstrator
levers.review_lever: This lever introduces the trade-off between technical credibility and procurement influence, but leaves unaddressed the gap in how this new approach will be integrated into existing systems.

levers.name: Engage with EU Digital Identity Associations
levers.consequences: This lever directly impacts EU-level engagement, ensuring that Danish advocacy aligns with broader European requirements on interoperability and reduced platform lock-in.
options: Collaborate with the European Digital Identity Association to create harmonized standards
Develop a joint position paper on platform-neutral access with other EU member states
Engage in technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework
levers.review_lever: This lever introduces the trade-off between EU-level engagement and domestic influence, but leaves unaddressed the gap in how this new approach will be integrated into existing national policies.

levers.name: Build a Coalition of Stakeholders
levers.consequences: This lever creates a coalition of stakeholders that can support platform-neutral and fallback-capable solutions, but may introduce conflicting interests and priorities.
options: Collaborate with civil society organizations to promote digital sovereignty
Develop a joint position paper on platform-neutrality with industry experts
Engage in advocacy work with sympathetic members of the Folketinget's digital policy committee
levers.review_lever: This lever introduces the trade-off between coalition-building and conflicting interests, but leaves unaddressed the gap in how this new approach will be integrated into existing decision-making processes.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — identify the primary trade-off this lever introduces, then state the specific gap the three options leave unaddressed.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season adds a fixed cost that erases the per-unit savings unless utilization reaches year-round levels."
     - "Each additional clinical site requires its own IRB approval, site-initiation visit, and staff credentialing — a sequential overhead that compounds rather than parallelizes, so doubling site count does not halve enrollment time."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions reduces expected annual loss on paper, but a single regional hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously, turning the diversification assumption into a concentration risk at the worst possible moment."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Length Limits**
   - Keep each `review_lever` to one sentence (20–40 words). State the trade-off and the gap concisely.
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Denmark's national digital identity infrastructure currently depends in practice on Apple iOS and Google Android for broad citizen access to essential services such as banking, tax filing, healthcare portals, and public administration. This dependency is not limited to app-store distribution. MitID's anti-fraud and app-integrity model relies on platform-controlled signals concerning approved installation channels and device integrity, which means that critical parts of the trust model for Danish digital identity are mediated by two foreign commercial platform providers. Even where direct observation of specific authentication events is not publicly documented, the current setup leaves Danish digital identity structurally dependent on platform vendors that control app availability, device trust signals, and surrounding telemetry environments. This creates not only a digital-sovereignty problem, but also a resilience and contingency problem: essential public access depends on a narrow supplier base and lacks a clearly platform-neutral fallback path.

This matters because Denmark's public-sector digital strategy for 2026–2029 explicitly calls for reduced dependence on a small number of large foreign technology suppliers and for stronger digital sovereignty. The issue should therefore not be framed only as privacy or ideology, but as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration, reduced strategic autonomy, continuity-of-service exposure, and the absence of a robust fallback authentication path if platform policies, integrity APIs, distribution terms, or geopolitical conditions change.

This project does not attempt to build and certify a production-ready replacement for MitID within the current trust framework. That would be unrealistic. The MitID ecosystem is conservative, certification-heavy, and tightly coupled to Apple/Google platform integrity signals. Instead, the project's objective is to establish the technical, regulatory, and political basis for formal platform-neutral access requirements and a certified fallback authentication path for Danish digital identity. The primary intervention point is not retrofitting MitID itself, but influencing AltID — Denmark's staged eIDAS2 wallet rollout — together with related procurement, standards, and continuity-planning decisions before the next identity stack hardens the same dependencies.

The project is structured in three phases over 24 months with a total budget of 10.5 million DKK, gated across phases (3.0 million for phase one, 4.0 million for phase two, 3.5 million for phase three). The higher budget reflects the need for credible technical work, policy engagement, external review, institutional overhead, and contingency capacity. Do not assume that 8 million DKK is sufficient for the same scope.

Phase one (months 1–8) is a feasibility, risk, and coalition-building phase. The technical work maps the relevant MitID dependency surface, especially platform integrity assumptions, app-distribution constraints, and the architectural conditions required for a platform-neutral or fallback trust model under eIDAS and NSIS at level "Substantial" or higher. The policy work reframes the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability. In parallel, the advocacy work builds a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee. The deliverables are: (1) a published feasibility and risk report, (2) a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, (3) a fallback-authentication concept note oriented toward AltID and future procurement requirements, and (4) a permit/approval and stakeholder matrix with lead times, decision gates, and identified blockers. Phase one funding is unconditional.

Phase two (months 9–16) develops non-production technical demonstrators. These are not certified MitID clients and do not attempt production integration with live MitID systems. The main demonstrator authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable privacy-respecting Android-compatible platform. A secondary demonstrator explores a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether. The goal is not to prove immediate deployability, but to produce credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based, fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google as trust anchors. Keep the demonstrator scope lean and defensible; do not expand into feature-rich product development. Phase two funding is conditional on phase one producing a published feasibility report, at least one formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or a related public-sector stakeholder, and a documented financing plan covering the remainder of the project.

Phase three (months 17–24) focuses on procurement, standards, and continuity intervention around AltID and related digital identity planning. Using the feasibility report, demonstrators, and coalition built in earlier phases, the project seeks inclusion of requirements stating that Danish digital identity systems should not depend exclusively on Apple or Google app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push-notification infrastructure, and should include a platform-neutral or otherwise certified fallback access path for critical public authentication. The project also engages with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework, with the aim of aligning Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. The project's role is not to dictate architecture directly, but to create technical evidence, procurement language, and standards pressure that make supplier-neutral and fallback-capable design harder to exclude. If formal traction with AltID is weak by month 16, phase three should de-scope toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input rather than pretending Danish procurement influence has been secured.

The project team should include a lead researcher in mobile security and authentication protocols, a regulatory and compliance specialist familiar with Danish and EU digital identity frameworks (including eIDAS and NSIS), a policy and public-affairs coordinator experienced in Danish digital governance and procurement processes, a technical lead for the demonstrator work, and at least one additional engineer or contractor covering web authentication, hardware-token workflows, or Linux/GrapheneOS-related implementation. The technical staffing should avoid dependence on a single specialist. Assume the project's primary physical base is in Copenhagen, Denmark, at a Danish university, independent research institution, or security-qualified R&D facility providing access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings. Budget for institutional overhead, external security review, and limited legal/procurement support explicitly rather than treating them as absorbed costs.

Success criteria: by month 24, the project has produced a published feasibility and risk report on platform-neutral and fallback-capable Danish digital identity; delivered two working non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback); secured a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or another relevant authority to the project's proposal; and achieved inclusion of platform-neutrality, fallback access, continuity, resilience, or supplier-concentration language in at least one official AltID-related design, policy, consultation, or procurement document. A stretch goal is that the project's EU-level engagement contributes to language in relevant European Digital Identity Wallet materials that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms. Do not use consumer adoption rates as a primary KPI for this project, because it is not shipping a production identity product.

Pick a realistic scenario that accounts for institutional inertia, regulatory friction, incumbent resistance, financing uncertainty, and the likelihood that convenience arguments will be used against platform-neutral alternatives. Do not assume constructive cooperation from existing operators. The primary lever is not technical disruption for its own sake, but the combination of resilience framing, procurement intervention, standards pressure, and credible technical demonstration.
